title: Register and Memory Reference
description: Complete reference for registers, memory layout, and addressing modes

sections.title: Architecture Overview
sections.content: DemiEngine features a rich register architecture with 134 total registers: 8 general-purpose registers (R0-R7), 8 extended registers (R8-R15), special registers (PC, SP, FP), and 100+ system registers. The CPU uses a stack-based memory model with support for both register-to-register and memory operations.

sections.title: General-Purpose Registers
sections.content:
**R0-R7: Primary Registers (32-bit)**

These are the main working registers available in standard mode:

- **R0**: Accumulator, function return values
- **R1**: First parameter, comparison operand
- **R2**: Second parameter
- **R3**: Third parameter
- **R4**: Local variables
- **R5**: Local variables
- **R6**: Local variables
- **R7**: Temporary values, frame pointer

**Register Encoding:**
```hex
00  # R0
01  # R1
02  # R2
03  # R3
04  # R4
05  # R5
06  # R6
07  # R7
```

sections.title: Extended Registers
sections.content:
**R8-R15: Extended Registers (32-bit)**

Available with the `--extended-registers` flag:

- **R8-R15**: Additional general-purpose registers
- Same capabilities as R0-R7
- Useful for complex algorithms requiring more registers
- Not commonly used in standard programs

**Usage:**
```bash
./bin/demi-engine --extended-registers program.hex
./bin/demi-engine -er program.hex
```

**Note:** Most programs use only R0-R7 for compatibility.

sections.title: Special Registers
sections.content:
**PC (Program Counter)**
- Holds address of current instruction
- Automatically incremented after each instruction
- Modified by jump and call instructions
- Read-only in most contexts

**SP (Stack Pointer)**
- Points to top of stack
- Automatically managed by PUSH/POP
- Decreases on PUSH (stack grows downward)
- Increases on POP
- Can be manually adjusted (advanced use)

**FP (Frame Pointer)**
- Used for stack frame management
- Must be manually managed
- Typically set at function entry
- Useful for accessing parameters and locals

**Flags Register**
- Contains status flags from operations
- Modified by arithmetic and compare instructions
- Read by conditional jumps

sections.title: CPU Flags
sections.content:
**Zero Flag (ZF)**
- Set when result equals zero
- Used by: JZ, JNZ
- Set by: CMP, arithmetic operations

**Carry Flag (CF)**
- Set on unsigned overflow (32-bit)
- Used by: JC, JNC
- Set by: ADD, SUB

**Sign Flag (SF)**
- Set when result is negative
- Used by: JS, JNS
- Set by: arithmetic operations, CMP

**Overflow Flag (OF)**
- Set on signed overflow
- Used by: JO, JNO
- Set by: ADD, SUB, MUL

**Example:**
```hex
# Compare and jump
0E 00 01            # CMP R0, R1  ; Sets flags
0F 10 00 00 00      # JZ target   ; Jump if equal (ZF=1)
```

sections.title: Register Usage Conventions
sections.content:
**Function Parameters:**
- R0: First parameter / Return value
- R1: Second parameter
- R2: Third parameter
- R3: Fourth parameter

**Preserved Registers (Callee-Save):**
- R4, R5, R6: Should be saved/restored by function
- Functions must preserve these if used

**Scratch Registers (Caller-Save):**
- R0, R1, R2, R3, R7: Can be modified by function
- Caller must save if needed after call

**Example Function:**
```hex
my_function:
    0A 04               # PUSH R4      ; Save R4
    04 04 00            # MOV R4, R0   ; Use R4
    02 04 01            # ADD R4, R1   ; Compute
    04 00 04            # MOV R0, R4   ; Return value
    0B 04               # POP R4       ; Restore R4
    1B                  # RET
```

sections.title: Memory Layout
sections.content:
```
High Memory  ┌─────────┐ 0xFFFFFFFF
            │  Stack  │ ← SP (grows down)
            ├─────────┤
            │  Free   │ ← Available for heap
            ├─────────┤
            │  Heap   │ ← Dynamic allocations
            ├─────────┤
            │  Data   │ ← Globals, strings
            ├─────────┤
Low Memory  │  Code   │ ← PC starts here (0x00)
            └─────────┘
```

**Memory Segments:**

- **Code Segment**: Program instructions (read-only)
- **Data Segment**: Constants, strings (initialized data)
- **Heap Segment**: Dynamic memory (manual management)
- **Stack Segment**: Function calls, local variables (automatic)

sections.title: Addressing Modes
sections.content:
**Immediate Addressing:**
```hex
01 00 2A 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 42  ; Load constant
```

**Register Addressing:**
```hex
04 00 01            # MOV R0, R1       ; Copy register
02 00 01            # ADD R0, R1       ; Register operation
```

**Direct Memory Addressing:**
```hex
06 00 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R0, [0x10]  ; Load from address
07 20 00 00 00 00    # STORE [0x20], R0 ; Store to address
```

**Indirect Addressing:**
```hex
01 01 10 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R1, 0x10  ; R1 = address
06 00 01            # LOAD R0, [R1]      ; Load from [R1]
07 01 00            # STORE [R1], R0     ; Store to [R1]
```

**Effective Address:**
```hex
20 00 10 00 00 00    # LEA R0, 0x10  ; R0 = address (not contents)
```

sections.title: Stack Architecture
sections.content:
**Stack Characteristics:**
- Grows downward (SP decreases on push)
- 32-bit aligned
- Managed by SP register
- No automatic bounds checking

**Stack Operations:**
```hex
# Push value
0A 00               # PUSH R0      ; [SP] = R0, SP -= 4

# Pop value
0B 00               # POP R0       ; R0 = [SP], SP += 4

# Push flags
1E                  # PUSH_FLAG    ; Save flags

# Pop flags
1F                  # POP_FLAG     ; Restore flags
```

**Function Call Stack:**
```
High    ┌──────────┐
        │ Arg 3    │ FP+16
        ├──────────┤
        │ Arg 2    │ FP+12
        ├──────────┤
        │ Arg 1    │ FP+8
        ├──────────┤
        │ Return   │ FP+4
        ├──────────┤
        │ Old FP   │ ← FP
        ├──────────┤
        │ Local 1  │ FP-4
        ├──────────┤
Low     │ Local 2  │ ← SP
        └──────────┘
```

sections.title: Data Types and Sizes
sections.content:
**Byte (8-bit):**
- Size: 1 byte
- Range: 0-255 (unsigned), -128 to 127 (signed)
- Used for: Characters, small integers

**Word (16-bit):**
- Size: 2 bytes
- Range: 0-65535 (unsigned), -32768 to 32767 (signed)
- Used for: Short integers

**Long/Dword (32-bit):**
- Size: 4 bytes
- Range: 0-4294967295 (unsigned), -2147483648 to 2147483647 (signed)
- Default register size
- Used for: Integers, addresses, pointers

**Qword (64-bit):**
- Size: 8 bytes
- Partially implemented
- Used for: Large integers

**String:**
- Null-terminated byte array
- Each character: 1 byte (ASCII)
- Example: `48 65 6C 6C 6F 00` = "Hello\0"

sections.title: Memory Operation Examples
sections.content:
**Loading Values:**
```hex
# Load immediate
01 00 2A 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 42

# Load from memory
06 00 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R0, [0x10]

# Load indirect
01 01 10 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R1, 0x10
06 00 01            # LOAD R0, [R1]
```

**Storing Values:**
```hex
# Store direct
01 00 2A 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 42
07 10 00 00 00 00    # STORE [0x10], R0

# Store indirect
01 01 10 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R1, 0x10
07 01 00            # STORE [R1], R0
```

**Swapping:**
```hex
# Swap register with memory
21 00 10 00 00 00    # SWAP R0, [0x10]
# R0 and memory[0x10] exchanged
```

sections.title: Byte Order (Endianness)
sections.content:
DemiEngine uses **little-endian** byte order:

**Example: 0x12345678 in memory**
```
Address  Value
0x00:    78  (least significant byte first)
0x01:    56
0x02:    34
0x03:    12  (most significant byte last)
```

**LOAD_IMM Encoding:**
```hex
01 00 2A 00 00 00
│  │  │  └──┴──┴── Padding (3 bytes)
│  │  └────────── Immediate value (8-bit)
│  └───────────── Register (8-bit)
└──────────────── Opcode (8-bit)
```

**Multi-byte Addresses:**
```hex
05 34 12 00 00
│  │  │  └──┴── High bytes (padding)
│  │  └──────── Address high byte (0x12)
│  └─────────── Address low byte (0x34)
└────────────── Opcode (JMP)

Jumps to: 0x1234 (little-endian)
```

sections.title: Memory Alignment
sections.content:
**Alignment Requirements:**

- **Byte access**: No alignment required
- **Word access**: 2-byte aligned (address % 2 == 0)
- **Dword access**: 4-byte aligned (address % 4 == 0)
- **Stack**: Always 4-byte aligned

**Best Practices:**
```hex
# Good: Aligned data
.org 0x10           # Start at aligned address
48 65 6C 6C 6F 00    # "Hello\0" (6 bytes)
00 00               # Padding to align next data

.org 0x18           # Next dword at aligned address
2A 00 00 00         # Integer 42
```

**Unaligned Access:**
- May cause performance penalty
- Or hardware exceptions (platform-dependent)
- Use byte operations for unaligned data

sections.title: Limitations and Gotchas
sections.content:
**LOAD_IMM 8-bit Limit:**
```hex
# Can only load 0-255
01 00 2A 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 42  ✓
01 00 FF 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 255 ✓
# 01 00 00 01 00 00  # Cannot load 256  ✗

# Workaround for larger values:
01 00 FF 00 00 00    # LOAD_IMM R0, 255
02 00 00            # ADD R0, R0       ; R0 = 510
```

**Carry Flag (32-bit):**
- Requires true 32-bit overflow
- Difficult to trigger intentionally
- Use NOT + ADD technique for testing

**Stack Overflow:**
- No automatic bounds checking
- Deep recursion can overwrite other memory
- Monitor SP in complex programs

**Register Encoding:**
- Only R0-R7 available without extended mode
- R8-R15 require `--extended-registers` flag
- Check compatibility before using extended registers

sections.title: Performance Optimization
sections.content:
**Register vs Memory:**
```hex
# Fast: Register operations
02 00 01            # ADD R0, R1  (1 cycle)

# Slower: Memory operations
06 02 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R2, [0x10]  (3+ cycles)
02 00 02            # ADD R0, R2       (1 cycle)
```

**Cache Values:**
```hex
# Bad: Repeated memory access
06 00 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R0, [0x10]
02 00 01            # ADD R0, R1
07 10 00 00 00 00    # STORE [0x10], R0
06 00 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R0, [0x10]  ; Unnecessary!

# Good: Keep in register
06 00 10 00 00 00    # LOAD R0, [0x10]
02 00 01            # ADD R0, R1
02 00 02            # ADD R0, R2       ; Use R0 directly
07 10 00 00 00 00    # STORE [0x10], R0
```

**Minimize Stack Use:**
- Use registers when possible
- Only save registers you'll modify
- Avoid excessive PUSH/POP in loops

sections.title: System Registers
sections.content:
**Extended Register Set (134 total):**

Beyond R0-R15, DemiEngine includes 100+ system registers for advanced features:

- **Control registers**: System configuration
- **Debug registers**: Breakpoints, tracing
- **Performance counters**: Profiling, metrics
- **Device registers**: Hardware interface

**Viewing System Registers:**
```bash
# Enable extended register display
./bin/demi-engine --extended-registers program.hex
./bin/demi-engine -er program.hex
```

**Note:** System registers are primarily for internal use and advanced debugging. Standard programs use only R0-R7.
